narrative:
The stress score has dropped sharply from 26 to 13, moving the regime firmly into calm territory as equity momentum and credit conditions aligned to the downside of risk. High-yield spreads at 261 basis points sit at the first percentile of their five-year range, signalling that credit markets are pricing virtually no distress, while the VIX at 16.4 in a normal contango structure confirms that options markets see no near-term volatility catalyst.

The standout move is the divergence between energy and emerging markets: XLE has shed 5.9% over five days while EEM has gained 5.4%, suggesting a rotation out of commodity-linked domestic exposure and into broader EM growth plays, with Japan adding further weight to the risk-on tone via a 5.0% advance in EWJ. A reversal in that EM momentum, or a credit spread widening that pushes HY OAS back above 300 basis points, would be the clearest signal that the current calm regime is coming under pressure.
